nativeRange.habitat: Pectinia is found on turbid reef slopes, in lagoons, and across protected reef zones, where it tends to tolerate lower light and sediment-rich conditions more readily than many other stony corals.

diet.notes: Like all corals, Pectinia meets most of its energy needs through zooxanthellae — photosynthetic algae living within its tissue — but it also benefits from supplemental target feeding one to three times per week. Suitable foods include mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine seafood, and coral-specific prepared foods. Pectinia tends to be a more active feeder at night, and has been documented capturing and consuming meaty foods with surprising effectiveness. Turning off pumps and powerheads during feeding allows food to settle on the polyps rather than being swept away.

avoidWith: other aggressive LPS corals placed without adequate spacing
any coral within sweeper tentacle reach (15+ cm clearance needed)
strong direct flow equipment positioned toward the coral

imageCandidates.notes: Debra Baker (via iNaturalist/GBIF), 1536x2048 — Pectinia paeonia in situ with vivid green/teal fluorescent ridges and maroon center; most striking colour in the pool, whole colony framed
Bernard DUPONT, 4060x2436 — sharp in-situ Pectinia paeonia laminar plate showing the diagnostic ruffled ridge/valley skeleton; tan/grey daylight colour
Chaloklum Diving, 834x750 — cluster of Pectinia lactuca colonies in situ; adds a second species to the genus entry, lower resolution
Chaloklum Diving, 1000x750 — Pectinia lactuca colonies in situ showing the crisped plate margins; slight underwater haze

summary: Pectinia corals are large polyp stony (LPS) corals known for their dramatic, ruffled plate-like skeletons and vivid coloration. Generally considered intermediate to keep, they tolerate lower light than most LPS corals but require stable water chemistry, moderate indirect flow, and generous spacing from neighbors to account for their sweeper tentacles.
careNotes:
Pectinia is discussed at the genus level in the aquarium trade because the roughly ten recognized species share near-identical husbandry requirements. Commonly sold trade morphs include the 'Space Invader Pectinia' (color forms of P. alcicornis) and the traditional 'Spiny Cup' form. The Space Invader morph has been reported to be more aggressive, with sweeper tentacle reach that can exceed that of standard morphs — allow at least 20 cm of clearance for those.

Lighting: Pectinia tends to thrive at moderate PAR (75–150). Unlike many LPS corals, it naturally inhabits more turbid, lower-light reef zones, so it tolerates and often prefers conditions that would be considered dim for other stony corals. New specimens should be acclimated gradually over 2–3 weeks to avoid bleaching from light shock.

Flow: Moderate, indirect flow is generally advised. Direct laminar flow against the fleshy tissue can tear or dislodge the coral, while flow that is too low allows detritus to settle in the valleys between the ridges, leading to tissue recession. Aim for gentle oscillating movement that keeps the polyps swaying without overwhelming them.

Aggression: Sweeper tentacles commonly extend 5–10 cm at night to capture food and defend territory. Maintain at least 15 cm of clear space around all edges of the coral.

Water chemistry: Stability is generally prioritized over absolute precision. Alkalinity swings of more than 0.5 dKH per day have been linked to tissue recession. Consistent calcium (400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–10 dKH), and magnesium (1,250–1,350 ppm) support healthy skeletal growth.

Nutrients: Moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 5–15 ppm, phosphate 0.03–0.10 ppm) appear to support better coloration and growth than ultra-low-nutrient systems.

Pectinia is generally considered intermediate rather than beginner-friendly, but is achievable in a stable, established reef without the extreme precision demands of small polyp stony (SPS) corals.

Brown jelly disease and tissue recession are the most commonly reported health issues, and both are typically triggered by physical damage, parameter swings, or excessive flow.

Fragging (cutting frags from a colony) is possible with a coral saw or bone cutters; cut pieces readily encrust and grow on frag plugs.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting) is the primary propagation method in captivity. Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a coral bandsaw; small frags encrust on frag plugs and grow readily under the same conditions as the parent colony. Sexual reproduction on natural reefs follows the standard broadcast spawning pattern for Scleractinia, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics – Complete Pectinia Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (75–77°F / 23.9–25°C), calcium (400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–10 dKH), magnesium (1250–1350 ppm), PAR (80–150), flow (gentle-medium), placement (middle-lower), temperament (aggressive sweeper tentacles). Agreed closely with Extreme Corals on all high-stakes parameters.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Care Requirements of Pectinia Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76–80°F, salinity SG 1.024–1.026, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, PAR 80–150. Temperature upper bound (80°F) is slightly higher than other sources; averaged to 26°C max.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn – ORA Pectinia Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (32–35 ppt = SG 1.025–1.027), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), calcium (350–450 ppm), magnesium (1250–1350 ppm). PAR suggestion (~50) is notably lower than other sources (80–150); likely reflects this ORA aquacultured strain's lower-light adaptation. PAR range set to 75–150 per majority consensus.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Pectinia (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for current accepted taxonomy (family Merulinidae, order Scleractinia) and genus-level species count (10 recognized species). LiveAquaria uses the outdated 'Pectiniidae'; Merulinidae reflects current molecular-based classification.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Pectinia Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked flow (moderate), PAR (~100), feeding frequency (2–3× weekly), temperament (generally non-aggressive except Space Invader morph which is 'very aggressive'). Confirmed family Merulinidae.
